description: This figure presents room-temperature engineering stress-strain curves (σ_E versus ε_E) for Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels after solution treatment, showing panels (a) and (b). Panel (a) displays curves for Fe30Mn11AlxC steels solution-treated at 1050°C for 2 h with carbon contents of 0.6C (black), 1.0C (red), 1.2C (green), and 1.5C (blue). Increasing carbon from 0.6% to 1.5% raises the yield strength from approximately 542 MPa to 1125 MPa but reduces tensile elongation from about 60% to 20%, accompanied by a transition from pronounced work hardening to work softening. Panel (b) shows curves for FexMn11Al1C steels under the same treatment with manganese contents of 20Mn (black), 25Mn (red), 30Mn (green), and 34Mn (blue); increasing Mn from 20% to 34% decreases the yield strength from about 896 MPa to 401 MPa while increasing total elongation from 28.5% to 60% and enhancing the work-hardening rate. The caption states that the complete Fig. 2 additionally includes panels (c) and (d) examining Al-content effects at 1050°C and 1100°C, respectively. Note that post-yield stress-strain jumps are caused by extensometer removal and do not affect mechanical measurements. These results demonstrate that C and Al additions strengthen the steel at the expense of ductility, whereas Mn enhances ductility but reduces strength, revealing key compositional trade-offs for property optimization.
